# **Advanced UAV Telemetry Parsing and Structure-from-Motion Pre-processing Architectures**

## **The Critical Role of Pre-processing in Modern Photogrammetry**

The integration of multi-rotor unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) into industrial and survey-grade mapping pipelines has fundamentally transformed the acquisition of high-precision geospatial data. Modern Structure-from-Motion (SfM) and Multi-View Stereo (MVS) algorithms, such as those powering COLMAP, OpenDroneMap (ODM), and Agisoft Metashape, rely on the extraction of high-dimensional feature vectors to compute complex 3D scene reconstructions. However, the computational stability and absolute geographic accuracy of these global bundle adjustments are heavily contingent upon the quality of the initial parameters fed into the system.1 Prior to feature extraction and matching, raw photographic datasets must undergo rigorous pre-processing to parse geospatial, orientation, and internal calibration metadata. The failure to correctly interpret this initial state data cascades through the photogrammetric pipeline, generating profound systematic errors, warping digital elevation models (DEMs), and corrupting the spatial integrity of the final orthomosaics.  
Historically, photogrammetric pipelines relied on standard Exchangeable Image File Format (EXIF) data, which encapsulates rudimentary GPS coordinates, generic focal lengths, and camera hardware identifiers.2 As the industry transitioned from qualitative aerial photography to quantitative surveying, standard EXIF proved critically inadequate. The conventional EXIF GPSAltitude tag, for instance, typically relies on uncalibrated barometric sensors, recording altitude relative to a variable takeoff location or a generic mean sea level (MSL) geoid, rather than a geodetically rigorous ellipsoidal datum.2 To circumvent these limitations, enterprise UAV manufacturers—most notably DJI and Autel Robotics—began embedding complex flight telemetry deep within custom Extensible Metadata Platform (XMP) namespaces.  
XMP data structures inject a highly granular spatial context into the image file at the exact microsecond of sensor exposure.2 This includes not merely longitude and latitude, but instantaneous spatial velocities, multi-axis gimbal kinematics, flight controller attitude, and Real-Time Kinematic (RTK) phase-carrier resolution flags. A comprehensive pre-processing architecture must reliably extract this proprietary XML-based telemetry, mathematically translate the recorded perspectives into projective geometries, and rigorously format the output to meet the highly specific, and often idiosyncratic, database schemas of engines like COLMAP and OpenDroneMap.

## **UAV Metadata Architectures: Resolving EXIF and XMP Discrepancies**

The architectural divergence between standard EXIF tags and proprietary manufacturer XMP namespaces necessitates highly specialized parsing logic. A robust pre-processing application cannot rely on generic image readers; it must explicitly query and prioritize custom metadata blocks to retrieve the true flight state of the UAV.

### **The DJI Custom Namespace and TimeSync Synchronization**

DJI has established a well-documented custom XMP namespace, officially designated as http://www.dji.com/drone-dji/1.0/, which injects over thirty proprietary telemetry fields directly into the header of every JPEG or DNG file.2 For spatial reconstruction, the most vital components of this namespace are the high-precision coordinate tags (drone-dji:GpsLatitude, drone-dji:GpsLongitude) and the dual altitude records (drone-dji:AbsoluteAltitude, drone-dji:RelativeAltitude).2 The absolute altitude tag records the height above a geodetic datum, whereas the relative altitude measures displacement exclusively from the drone's localized home point.2  
In advanced models such as the Phantom 4 RTK and Mavic 3 Enterprise, this metadata is further refined by DJI’s proprietary TimeSync system.5 TimeSync was engineered to continuously align the internal clocks of the flight controller, the imaging payload, and the RTK positioning module at the microsecond level.5 Crucially, TimeSync applies a dynamic compensation value to the coordinates before they are written to the XMP block, mathematically shifting the reported GPS position from the physical phase center of the upper RTK antenna down to the exact optical center of the CMOS image sensor.5 This lever-arm correction guarantees that the positional metadata reflects the true nodal point of the camera projection, optimizing the initialization of the photogrammetric bundle adjustment.5

### **Fragmentation and Anomalies in the Autel Robotics XMP Schema**

While DJI maintains relative consistency across its enterprise fleet, Autel Robotics presents a highly fragmented metadata ecosystem, complicated by shifting firmware standards and pervasive typographical errors within the XMP injection scripts.8 Extracting reliable telemetry from models like the Autel EVO II RTK requires conditional parsing logic based on the specific firmware version detected in the image header.8  
Older Autel firmware models encapsulate orientation data using entirely different proprietary tags, specifically Camera:Pitch, Camera:Yaw, and Camera:Roll.8 Furthermore, these legacy firmware builds employ a non-standard angular mapping for the camera pitch. In standard photogrammetry, a nadir perspective (pointing straight down at the earth) is mathematically defined as ![][image1], while the horizon is ![][image2].4 Older Autel systems inverted this convention, defining nadir as ![][image2] and the horizon as ![][image3].8 Consequently, pre-processing software must detect legacy Autel hardware and apply a geometric transformation (![][image4]) to the pitch values before projecting the camera frustum.8  
Newer Autel firmware updates attempted to standardize the metadata by mimicking the DJI namespace layout, shifting to a drone: prefix.8 However, this transition introduced a notorious typographical error into the schema. Autel’s metadata generation script frequently mislabels the longitudinal coordinate as drone:GpsLongtitude instead of the correct drone:GpsLongitude.8 If a Python parsing script utilizes strict string matching for the correct spelling, it will return null values for the drone's position, completely derailing the SfM pipeline.8 Robust extraction algorithms must therefore implement fallback searches or fuzzy matching for the "Longtitude" anomaly.8

| Parameter Concept | DJI Firmware XMP Tag | Autel Firmware XMP Tag (New) | Autel Firmware XMP Tag (Legacy) |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Latitude** | drone-dji:GpsLatitude | drone:GpsLatitude | Standard EXIF GPSLatitude |
| **Longitude** | drone-dji:GpsLongitude | drone:GpsLongtitude (Typo) | Standard EXIF GPSLongitude |
| **Absolute Altitude** | drone-dji:AbsoluteAltitude | drone:AbsoluteAltitude | EXIF GPSAltitude (Barometric) |
| **Gimbal Pitch** | drone-dji:GimbalPitchDegree | drone:GimbalPitchDegree | Camera:Pitch (Requires ![][image5]) |
| **Gimbal Yaw** | drone-dji:GimbalYawDegree | drone:GimbalYawDegree | Camera:Yaw |
| **Gimbal Roll** | drone-dji:GimbalRollDegree | drone:GimbalRollDegree | Camera:Roll |

### **Programmatic Extraction Methodologies in Python**

Relying exclusively on standard metadata wrappers like pyexiv2 can sometimes fail when confronted with malformed or nested manufacturer tags. While utilizing an automated wrapper around Phil Harvey’s ExifTool is highly reliable for surface-level tags, deep XMP extraction often necessitates reading the image file as a raw binary object in Python. The optimal approach involves opening the file via standard byte-reading (open(filepath, 'rb')), searching the binary stream for the exact string markers \<x:xmpmeta\> and \</x:xmpmeta\>, and isolating the raw XML payload.8  
Once the raw XMP string is isolated, the script can leverage native XML parsing libraries (such as xml.etree.ElementTree) or execute targeted string manipulation to bypass XML namespace conflicts. By slicing the string immediately following identifiers like drone-dji:AbsoluteAltitude=" and terminating at the next quotation mark, the software can reliably pull the exact float values regardless of XMP header corruption.8 This extraction logic must explicitly prioritize the XMP absolute altitude over the EXIF altitude to ensure that subsequent processing engines receive RTK-corrected ellipsoidal heights rather than barometric estimations.2

## **GNSS Solution Precision and RTK Telemetry Validation**

The initialization phase of a bundle adjustment algorithm utilizes the geographic coordinates of each camera as rigid anchors. The spatial uncertainty of these coordinates directly dictates the speed of the optimization and the probability of the algorithm converging on a local minimum rather than the global optimal structure. Modern survey UAVs utilize dual-band RTK receivers to achieve centimeter-level positioning accuracy, drastically reducing the reliance on physically surveyed Ground Control Points (GCPs).5 However, the physical presence of an RTK antenna on the UAV chassis does not guarantee survey-grade precision.

### **Phase-Carrier Ambiguity and Signal Degradation**

RTK positioning relies on resolving the carrier-phase ambiguities between the satellite signals received by the drone and the identical signals received by a static base station (such as the D-RTK 2 mobile station) or an NTRIP internet broadcaster.5 When line-of-sight to the satellites is obstructed by urban canyons, dense canopy, or significant electromagnetic interference, the receiver may fail to resolve the integer ambiguities.5 In these scenarios, the GNSS solution degrades from a high-precision "Fixed" state to a "Float" state, or drops entirely to an uncorrected autonomous GPS solution.10  
If images captured during a period of signal degradation are ingested into the SfM pipeline without weighting their increased uncertainty, the resulting 3D model will exhibit severe localized warping.11 Consequently, the pre-processing script must parse the RTK accuracy flags embedded within the XMP metadata to validate the GNSS precision of every single photograph in the dataset.

### **Parsing and Evaluating the RtkFlag**

In the DJI metadata architecture, the phase-carrier solution status is recorded under the drone-dji:RtkFlag tag (and similarly under drone:RtkFlag for newer Autel models).4 This tag is stored as an integer value representing the exact state of the GNSS positioning engine at the moment the shutter was actuated.10

| RtkFlag Integer Value | GNSS Solution Classification | Expected Positional Accuracy | Photogrammetric Implication |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| 0 | Failed to Position | ![][image6] meters | RTK correction completely offline. |
| 16 | Single Point Positioning | ![][image7] meters | Standard GPS; base station connection lost. |
| 32 to 49 | RTK Float Solution | Decimeter to Meter level | Ambiguities unresolved; multipath errors present. |
| 50 | RTK Fixed Solution | ![][image7] centimeters | Optimal precision; high confidence for SfM anchoring. |

A robust pre-processing application must extract this integer for every image.10 If the script detects RtkFlag values falling into the 32\~49 range, it indicates a floating point solution where the positional data has drifted into decimeter-level inaccuracy.10 Values of 16 or 0 indicate complete reliance on autonomous meter-level GPS.10  
To provide nuanced warnings, the script must also parse the accompanying standard deviation fields: drone-dji:RtkStdLon (longitude standard deviation), drone-dji:RtkStdLat (latitude standard deviation), and drone-dji:RtkStdHgt (elevation standard deviation).10 These values provide a quantifiable metric of coordinate uncertainty in meters.10 The pre-processor should calculate the rolling average of these standard deviations across the flight block. If the spatial index detects a cluster of images where the mean standard deviation exceeds ![][image8] meters, or if the RtkFlag drops below 50, the desktop application must raise a severe warning.10 This prompt alerts the user that the dataset lacks the absolute precision required for survey deliverables, indicating that the user must either execute a Post-Processed Kinematic (PPK) workflow using the raw .obs RINEX files to recalculate the trajectory, or manually integrate traditional GCPs into the photogrammetry software to constrain the drift.5  
Furthermore, the XMP tag drone-dji:AltitudeType="RtkAlt" denotes that the altitude is recorded as an ellipsoidal height relative to the WGS84 datum, rather than an orthometric height relative to the EGM96 geoid.4 If the RtkFlag is absent or the hardware defaults to barometric altitude (common in older Autel systems), the python script must mathematically convert the EGM96 altitude into WGS84 using localized geoid undulation grids to ensure vertical consistency across multi-flight datasets.8

## **Geospatial Overlap Analysis via 2D Footprint Projection**

Calculating the geospatial overlap between consecutive photographs before initiating the SfM pipeline provides a massive computational advantage. Feature extraction (using algorithms like SIFT) and exhaustive feature matching are computationally expensive operations. If a dataset lacks the critical frontal overlap (typically ![][image9]) or lateral overlap (typically ![][image10]) required for robust photogrammetry, the bundle adjustment will fail, generating disconnected sub-models.15 Detecting insufficient overlap via 2D footprint projection prevents the expenditure of hours of GPU processing on inherently flawed datasets.  
This spatial analysis requires projecting the camera's optical frustum onto a ground plane and computing the Intersection over Union (IoU) of the resulting bounding polygons.15 The calculation demands the drone's geographic coordinates, absolute altitude above ground, the physical dimensions of the camera sensor, the focal length, and the instantaneous three-axis orientation of the gimbal.2

### **Mathematical Derivation of the Projection Frustum**

The projection algorithm begins by establishing the angular boundaries of the camera sensor. Using the focal length ![][image11] and the physical sensor dimensions (width ![][image12] and height ![][image13]) extracted from the EXIF metadata, the horizontal and vertical field of view angles (![][image14], ![][image15]) are computed via standard trigonometric relations:  
![][image16]  
These angles define four directional unit vectors originating from the camera's optical center, representing the four corners of the image frame in the camera's local coordinate system.16  
Before these vectors can be projected onto the earth, they must be rotated to match the physical attitude of the camera in the sky.17 The XMP metadata provides the instantaneous orientation of the gimbal via three tags: GimbalYawDegree (![][image17]), GimbalPitchDegree (![][image18]), and GimbalRollDegree (![][image19]).20 These values are referenced to a local North-East-Down (NED) coordinate system, which inherently dictates a ![][image20] intrinsic rotation sequence.18  
The global rotation matrix ![][image21] is derived by multiplying the individual primary axis rotation matrices 17:  
![][image22]  
Where the foundational matrices are defined as:  
![][image23]  
Multiplying the four local corner vectors by ![][image21] transforms them into direction vectors in the global NED frame.15 A common complexity arises from the pitch definition; a standard mapping flight orients the camera straight down, which DJI records as a pitch of ![][image1].4 This extreme pitch angle ensures the projected rays intersect the ground plane directly beneath the drone.15

### **Ray-Plane Intersection and Spatial Indexing in Python**

To determine the physical ground footprint, a ray-plane intersection algorithm is executed. Assuming the terrain is relatively flat compared to the flight altitude, the ground is mathematically modeled as the plane ![][image24] in a localized Cartesian coordinate system.15 The camera's optical center is positioned at ![][image25], where ![][image26] is the RelativeAltitude of the drone.2 Each of the four rotated corner vectors ![][image27] takes the form of a directional ray ![][image28]. The physical intersection point ![][image29] on the ground plane is determined by extending the ray until it satisfies the ![][image24] condition:  
![][image30]  
![][image31]  
To apply Euclidean geometry over vast distances, the drone's latitude and longitude must first be converted from the WGS84 geodetic system (EPSG:4326) into a local Cartesian projection, such as the Universal Transverse Mercator (UTM) system or an Azimuthal Equidistant (AEQD) projection.16 In Python, this is executed performantly using the pyproj library 16:

Python  
from pyproj import Proj, Transformer  
\# Transforming from WGS84 to a local UTM zone (e.g., EPSG:32632)  
transformer \= Transformer.from\_proj("epsg:4326", "epsg:32632", always\_xy=True)  
easting, northing \= transformer.transform(longitude, latitude)

The computed ![][image32] and ![][image33] offsets are added to the localized Easting and Northing coordinates. These four ground points are then instantiated as a shapely.geometry.Polygon object, representing the exact territorial footprint of the photograph.16  
To calculate the overlap across thousands of images, iterating through every possible image pair presents an ![][image34] computational bottleneck. To resolve this, a spatial index—specifically an R-Tree—must be implemented using libraries such as geopandas or rtree. The R-Tree indexes the minimal bounding boxes of all generated polygons. For any given image, the R-Tree executes a highly optimized spatial query to instantly return only the subset of geographically adjacent images. The exact intersection area is then calculated exclusively for these neighbors using Shapely’s polygon1.intersection(polygon2).area.22 Dividing this intersection area by the total area of a single footprint yields the precise overlap percentage, allowing the desktop application to map and flag regions of insufficient data density prior to SfM processing.

## **COLMAP Camera Model Integration and the Principal Point Convention**

When injecting pre-processed metadata and intrinsic calibrations into COLMAP—an industry-standard open-source SfM pipeline renowned for its dense reconstruction fidelity—the mathematical definition of the camera's internal geometry is heavily scrutinized.1 A fundamental structural discrepancy exists between how COLMAP defines the origin of the image coordinate system and how standard computer vision libraries, such as OpenCV, define it.24 Neglecting this discrepancy during database synthesis results in severe, systematic geometric errors during the reconstruction phase.26

### **The Half-Pixel Coordinate System Discrepancy**

In the OpenCV framework, the image plane is modeled using an integer pixel-center convention. In this architecture, the exact mathematical center of the top-left pixel in the image array is assigned the coordinate ![][image35].24 Conversely, COLMAP aligns its coordinate system with the physical boundaries of the imaging sensor. According to the sensor/models.h documentation within the COLMAP source code, the upper-left corner of the physical boundary of the top-left pixel is defined as ![][image35].24 Consequently, the geometric center of that top-left pixel is located at ![][image36].24  
This sub-pixel topological definition directly impacts the initialization of the Principal Point (![][image37]), which represents the coordinate where the optical axis intersects the image plane.26 If camera intrinsic parameters are extracted from OpenCV calibration routines or derived from manufacturer EXIF tags conforming to generalized standards, the principal point is defined relative to the OpenCV ![][image35] origin.25  
To convert an OpenCV calibration matrix into the specific numerical format required by COLMAP's cameras.txt file or its internal SQLite database, a strict mathematical shift of ![][image38] pixels must be applied to the principal point coordinates.25  
Given a standard OpenCV camera calibration matrix ![][image39]:  
![][image40]  
The corresponding parameters injected into the COLMAP architecture must be translated 25:  
![][image41]  
![][image42]

### **Propagation of the Sub-Pixel Shift Error**

Omitting the ![][image38] pixel translation may appear negligible on a per-image basis, particularly when processing modern 20-megapixel sensors where a single pixel represents a fraction of a millimeter. However, photogrammetry relies on the recursive minimization of reprojection errors across millions of feature tracks during global bundle adjustment.1 The bundle adjuster simultaneously optimizes the 3D point coordinates, the camera poses (extrinsics), and the camera calibrations (intrinsics).1  
If the principal point is artificially offset by half a pixel, the radial distortion models (such as the Brown-Conrady coefficients ![][image43]) become fundamentally decentered relative to the true optical axis.26 To minimize the resulting reprojection error, the optimizer artificially warps the camera extrinsics to compensate for the mathematically induced asymmetry. Across a linear flight path containing hundreds of drone images, this sub-pixel tension accumulates quadratically. This structural accumulation generates a pervasive error known as "doming" or "bowling," where the resulting digital elevation model (DEM) and point cloud exhibit a pronounced, artificial parabolic curvature.26 This sub-pixel coordinate shift must be explicitly hardcoded into any Python implementation synthesizing COLMAP inputs.  
Furthermore, when instantiating the OPENCV camera model in COLMAP, the parameter sequence is strictly enforced. The base OPENCV model expects exactly eight parameters in the following order: fx, fy, cx, cy, k1, k2, p1, p2.24 Advanced models like OPENCV\_FISHEYE or OPENCV\_3RADIAL append additional coefficients, but the foundational structure relies entirely on the correctly shifted ![][image44] and ![][image45] values.28

| Parameter Index | COLMAP OPENCV Model Parameter | Required Mathematical Shift (from OpenCV origin) |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| 0 | ![][image46] (Focal Length X) | None |
| 1 | ![][image47] (Focal Length Y) | None |
| 2 | ![][image44] (Principal Point X) | ![][image38] |
| 3 | ![][image45] (Principal Point Y) | ![][image38] |
| 4-7 | ![][image43] (Radial/Tangential Distortion) | None |

## **Direct SQLite Database Synthesis for COLMAP**

COLMAP operates by aggregating all extracted features, geospatial orientations, matching graphs, and camera models inside a centralized, highly relational SQLite database (database.db).29 In advanced desktop applications, Python is frequently utilized to bypass COLMAP's native feature extraction GUI, opting instead to directly inject pre-processed drone telemetry, shared intrinsic focal lengths, and explicitly defined camera models into the database via the standard sqlite3 module.29  
Synthesizing this database from scratch requires strict adherence to COLMAP's internal Binary Large Object (BLOB) schema and its relational constraints. The primary architectural foundation relies on the interaction between the cameras table and the images table.29 The cameras table defines the unique internal geometry of the sensors utilized during the mission, allowing a single camera parameter entry to be referenced via a camera\_id foreign key by thousands of individual entries in the images table.29

### **The cameras Table Schema and BLOB Packaging**

The schema utilized by COLMAP for the cameras table mandates the following structure: CREATE TABLE cameras (camera\_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT NOT NULL, model INTEGER NOT NULL, width INTEGER NOT NULL, height INTEGER NOT NULL, params BLOB, prior\_focal\_length INTEGER NOT NULL).32  
The params column poses the most significant risk for implementation failure.33 The COLMAP engine explicitly expects the camera intrinsic parameters to be stored as a contiguous binary blob of float64 (double-precision floating-point) values.29 When constructing this blob in Python, naive string casting, standard 32-bit float serialization, or loose array typing will corrupt the SQL entry. When the COLMAP executable is subsequently launched to perform feature matching or bundle adjustment, it will fail to read the corrupted memory block, resulting in an immediate segmentation fault or processing crash.33  
To serialize this blob securely, Python’s numpy library should be utilized to guarantee strict byte alignment and bit-depth configuration. The parameters must be cast into an array of type numpy.float64 and translated into binary via the tobytes() method.36  
Alternatively, the Python struct module provides low-level control over binary packing. However, endianness pitfalls must be carefully navigated.30 COLMAP expects little-endian representation on standard x86 and ARM architectures. Utilizing struct.pack('\<8d', \*params\_array) explicitly enforces little-endian (\<) double-precision (d) formatting for an eight-parameter array.30 If the data is accidentally packed as big-endian (\>), COLMAP will parse the reversed byte order as arbitrary astronomical or infinitesimal numbers, causing immediate structural failure during geometry validation.30

### **The prior\_focal\_length Heuristic**

The prior\_focal\_length column functions as a boolean-like integer flag (0 or 1), fundamentally altering the behavior of the bundle adjustment optimizer.32 Setting this integer to 1 explicitly signals the reconstruction algorithm that the provided focal length is derived from a reliable, highly accurate source—such as factory calibration matrices embedded within DJI's XMP DewarpData tags, or a rigorous laboratory OpenCV checkerboard calibration.37  
Setting the flag to 0 removes constraints on the bundle adjuster, granting it vast degrees of freedom to aggressively warp the focal length to minimize reprojection errors.37 While this unconstrained optimization is beneficial when processing uncalibrated internet photos with missing metadata, it is highly detrimental for survey-grade drone mapping. Allowing the SfM engine to alter the focal length of a pre-calibrated RTK drone can absorb subtle radial distortions into the focal length parameter, introducing severe, systemic scale distortions into the final point cloud.37 For UAV pipelines parsing precise XMP telemetry, this flag must invariably be clamped to 1\.37

## **Camera Calibration Grouping for OpenDroneMap and Metashape**

In large-scale industrial mapping operations, it is common to deploy multiple distinct UAVs simultaneously (e.g., a DJI Mavic 3 Enterprise mapping alongside an Autel EVO II RTK) or to swap camera payloads on heavy-lift platforms like the Matrice 300 RTK.40 Even when executing multi-flight datasets using the exact same physical drone over several days, subtle physical shifts in the sensor's internal geometry—induced by ambient temperature fluctuations, mechanical flight vibrations, or simply powering the hardware down and back up—can alter the lens characteristics.43  
Photogrammetry engines such as OpenDroneMap (ODM) and Agisoft Metashape process these heterogeneous datasets by assigning images into "Camera Calibration Groups" (CCGs).41 Images placed within the same calibration group share a singular mathematical camera model, and their intrinsic parameters (focal length, principal point, distortion coefficients) are optimized collectively across the group.41 If images captured by different physical sensors, or captured under significantly different environmental conditions, are forced into a single CCG, the bundle adjustment algorithm will fail to converge on a unified solution, resulting in heavy ghosting effects, fractured orthomosaics, and corrupted digital surface models.41

### **Heuristics for Generating Unique Image Groups**

An advanced pre-processing application must autonomously cluster images into logically separated groups before passing the data to the SfM pipeline. The foundational EXIF tags utilized to define a unique calibration group are:

1. **Camera Make** (EXIF:Make)  
2. **Camera Model** (EXIF:Model)  
3. **Image Dimensions** (EXIF:ImageWidth, EXIF:ImageHeight)  
4. **Focal Length** (EXIF:FocalLength)

Relying strictly on these hardware tags, however, is insufficient for professional survey operations. Industry best practice mandates the generation of a distinct calibration group for any subset of images that display variations in collection date, operational flight altitude, or lateral flight speed.41 For example, photographs captured during a high-speed transect at ![][image48] meters per second will require entirely different rolling shutter compensation parameters than photographs captured during a slower, ![][image49] meters per second inspection pass.41  
While software like Metashape features an "Adaptive camera model fitting" option, photogrammetry experts strictly advise against relying on this adaptive fitting for multi-drone or multi-flight datasets.41 Instead, the groups must be split cleanly and explicitly, ensuring that interior orientation parameters are calculated independently for each hardware configuration, thereby maximizing the geometric fidelity of the reconstruction.41

### **Constructing the image\_groups.txt File for ODM**

OpenDroneMap facilitates direct manual intervention in this clustering process via the generation of an image\_groups.txt file.44 This text file is particularly critical when utilizing ODM's split-merge workflow, which is designed to process massive datasets (containing thousands of images) by dividing the reconstruction across distributed memory nodes.46  
If the pre-processing application identifies multiple distinct camera models or varied operational conditions based on the telemetry, it automatically generates the image\_groups.txt file and deposits it in the root dataset directory.45 The syntax of this file operates as a simplistic, case-sensitive associative map. It requires one line per image, separating the exact image filename and the designated group identifier with a space 45:  
DJI\_0001.JPG Group\_Mavic3E\_Flight1  
DJI\_0002.JPG Group\_Mavic3E\_Flight1  
AUTEL\_001.JPG Group\_EVO2\_Flight1  
AUTEL\_002.JPG Group\_EVO2\_Flight1  
By injecting this text file and passing the command-line argument \--split-overlap 0, ODM is instructed to bypass its internal clustering algorithms and strictly adhere to the calibration groups defined by the Python pre-processor.45  
This explicit grouping architecture is also a hard requirement when integrating Ground Control Points (GCPs) into an ODM split-merge workflow. When the pre-processor divides the dataset into submodels, it must guarantee that each distinct calibration group geographically encompasses at least three independent GCPs.46 If a submodel contains fewer than three GCPs, that specific hardware group cannot be rigidly anchored to the geographic datum. It will revert to relying solely on the localized EXIF GNSS data, severely degrading the absolute global accuracy of that specific sector of the final orthomosaic.46 By programmatically linking RTK validation, geospatial overlap analysis, and rigorous calibration grouping, the pre-processing architecture ensures computational efficiency and guarantees the mathematical stability of the final photogrammetric output.
